label: Abstract claim 1
verbatim: We argue that effective retraining-free compression should avoid updating expert parameters while allowing lightweight router calibration. To this end, we propose Router Knowledge Distillation (Router KD), which updates only a tiny fraction of parameters (the router) by distilling the original model's next-token distribution on unlabeled calibration data.
paraphrase: Core thesis: retraining-free MoE compression must couple expert compression with router-only calibration. Router KD distills the teacher's next-token distribution into the student router only.
preconditions: Compressed MoE model with frozen experts; unlabeled calibration corpus; access to original (teacher) model.
consequences: Restores performance lost to expert compression with negligible trainable parameters; redefines 'retraining-free' to allow router updates.

label: Abstract claim 2
verbatim: Experiments across representative methods in all three paradigms demonstrate consistent performance recovery, with substantially larger gains in fine-grained MoEs (many small experts) than in coarse-grained MoEs due to their more complex routing decision boundaries.
paraphrase: Router KD recovers performance across Pruning/Editing/Merging; gains are much larger on fine-grained (Qwen3, 128 experts) than coarse-grained (Mixtral, 8 experts) MoEs.
preconditions: Pre-trained MoE; expert compression already applied.
consequences: Architecture-dependent benefit: only fine-grained MoEs see strong recovery.

label: Taxonomy
verbatim: We organize retraining-free MoE compression into three paradigms—Expert Pruning, Expert Editing, and Expert Merging.
paraphrase: Three-paradigm taxonomy of retraining-free MoE compression.
preconditions: Parameter-level (not bit-level) compression; quantization explicitly excluded.
consequences: All subsequent analysis (Sec 3, 5) operates within these three paradigms.

label: Eq §3.1 (original MoE output)
verbatim: g̃_i = g_i / Σ_{j∈S} g_j , i ∈ S ;  y = Σ_{i∈S} g̃_i · E_i(x)
paraphrase: Original MoE renormalizes top-k gate scores g_i over selected set S then weighted-sums expert outputs.
preconditions: Top-k routing; |S|=k<n; experts E_0..E_n; gate scores g_0..g_n.
consequences: Baseline output for analyzing pruning/editing/merging deviations.

label: Renormalized weights (pruned)
verbatim: g̃^{orig,(A)}_i(x) = g^{orig}_i(x) / Σ_{j∈A} g^{orig}_j(x) ;  g̃^{pruned,(A)}_i(x) = g^{pruned}_i(x) / Σ_{j∈A} g^{pruned}_j(x), i ∈ A.
paraphrase: Renormalized gate weights over an arbitrary index set A for original vs pruned models.
consequences: Used to derive routing-discrepancy bounds.

label: Best-case pruning output diff
verbatim: |y_orig(x) − y^{best}_pruned(x)| = |Σ_{i∈S} (g̃^{orig,(S)}_i(x) − g̃^{pruned,(S)}_i(x)) E_i(x)|
paraphrase: Even when pruning preserves the originally selected expert set S, output still differs because router renormalization differs.
consequences: Proves router-induced error exists even in the best pruning scenario.

label: §3.2.2 router-amplification claim
verbatim: discrepancies induced by the router arise even in the rare best-case scenario. Although the router weight g̃_i is merely a scalar value between 0 and 1, the Expert itself is a matrix consisting of over 1 million scalar values; thus, the resulting deviation is by no means negligible.
paraphrase: Small router-weight differences amplify because experts are large matrices; deviation is non-negligible and grows in common/worst pruning cases.
preconditions: MoE expert is a matrix with ~10^6 parameters.
consequences: Motivates router calibration as primary recovery target.

label: Edited renormalized weights
verbatim: g̃^{edit,(A)}_i(x) = g^{edit}_i(x) / Σ_{j∈A} g^{edit}_j(x), i ∈ A.
paraphrase: Renormalized gate weights for the edited (decomposition-based) MoE.
preconditions: Editing preserves expert count N→N but modifies parameters P→P'.
consequences: Even with identical selected indices, gate scores shift because preceding-layer parameters changed.

label: Best-case editing output diff
verbatim: |y_orig(x) − y^{best}_edit(x)| = |Σ_{i∈S} (g̃^{orig,(S)}_i(x) E_i(x) − g̃^{edit,(S)}_i(x) X_i(x))|
paraphrase: Best-case editing keeps S^edit = S but both gate scores and expert functions (E vs X) differ.
preconditions: S^edit = S; X_i ≈ E_i but not identical.
consequences: Router mismatch persists even when edited experts approximate originals.

label: §3.3 mismatch-error dominates editing-error
verbatim: the error stemming from selecting an incorrect expert—specifically the difference between the original expert E_i and a mismatched edited expert X_j—exceeds the error introduced by the editing process itself (i.e., the difference between E_i and its corresponding X_i).
paraphrase: Picking wrong expert (E_i vs X_j) causes more error than editing-approximation error (E_i vs X_i); thus router calibration is more impactful than improving editing fidelity alone.
preconditions: Sparse MoE with distinctive experts in a layer.
consequences: If router selects X_i (correct) instead of X_j, output deviation is minimized.

label: §3.4 Merging analysis
verbatim: Expert Merging entails nine distinct scenarios. ... similar to Pruning and Editing, Expert Merging inevitably suffers from router-induced errors even in the best-case scenario.
paraphrase: Merging has 9 cases (vs 3 for pruning/editing); router-induced error is unavoidable, and most-common case worsens it.
preconditions: N → M experts via merging; original router targets pre-merge experts.
consequences: Router calibration needed for merging too.

label: Fig 2 empirical router drift
verbatim: Pruning models exhibit a relatively larger divergence in assigned probabilities compared to Editing and Merging. ... as the layers deepen, the router increasingly selects experts different from those intended by the original model.
paraphrase: Empirically (100 ELI5 samples on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507): pruning has highest L1 router-prob divergence; top-8 expert overlap drops with depth across all paradigms.
consequences: Confirms theoretical claim that compression compounds router error across layers.

label: Eq. 1 (Router-only KD objective)
verbatim: θ*_R = arg min_{θ_R} E_{x∼D_cal} [L_RKD(x; θ_T, θ_R)] .
paraphrase: Router-only optimization: minimize expected RKD loss over calibration corpus, updating only θ_R while θ_E (experts + shared blocks) is frozen.
preconditions: Teacher params θ_T fixed; student decomposed as θ_S = (θ_R, θ_E); D_cal unlabeled.
consequences: Establishes the Router-KD optimization problem.

label: Eq. 2 (softened next-token distribution)
verbatim: p^{(t)}_M(·) = Softmax(z^{(t)}_M / τ),  M ∈ {T,S}
paraphrase: Temperature-scaled softmax over vocabulary logits at position t; same τ>0 used for teacher and student.
consequences: Yields the soft targets used in Eq. 3.

label: Eq. 3 (Router KD loss; KL with τ²)
paraphrase: Per-sequence loss is mask-normalized, τ²-scaled forward-KL from teacher to student over predicted next tokens. Padding masked via m_{t+1}; N_x = Σ m_{t+1} + ε.
preconditions: Token sequence x = (x_1..x_L); loss mask m ∈ {0,1}; small ε>0.
consequences: Defines the exact KD loss; uses KL (not CE) and τ²-scaling consistent with Hinton-style distillation.

label: Gradient routing claim
verbatim: although the distillation loss is defined on the output token distribution, gradients are backpropagated and applied exclusively to the student router parameters θ_R, while all expert and backbone parameters remain frozen.
paraphrase: Loss is on output logits but updates only θ_R via gradients flowing through the soft gating weights of selected experts.
preconditions: Standard MoE forward pass with differentiable soft top-k weighting on selected experts.
consequences: Router-only update preserves expert structure; no expert/backbone change.

label: Logit-distill vs router-match rationale
verbatim: By distilling output logits rather than matching router gate values explicitly, Router KD avoids requiring the teacher and student to share identical expert sets or gate dimensionalities, which may not hold after pruning, editing, or merging.
paraphrase: KD on output logits (not on router gate values) lets compressed model have different expert count / gate dim than teacher.
preconditions: Teacher and student may have mismatched expert sets after compression.
consequences: General-purpose KD applicable across all three paradigms.

label: Router parameter fraction
verbatim: the router accounts for approximately 0.04% of parameters in Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507 and 0.002% in Mixtral-8×7B-Instruct-v0.1.
paraphrase: Trainable parameter share is tiny: 0.04% Qwen3, 0.002% Mixtral.
consequences: Justifies 'lightweight' label and short wall-clock.

label: Router KD wall-clock
verbatim: under the hyperparameter settings used in our experiments, Router KD required approximately 2 hours for Qwen3 and about 40 minutes for Mixtral.
paraphrase: Wall-clock for Router KD: ~2 h on Qwen3-30B, ~40 min on Mixtral-8×7B.
preconditions: Settings of Table 1 (1 epoch, bs=2, ga=4, lr=5e-5, max-len=512, T=1.0, ≤3000 calib samples). Qwen3 on A100 40GB, Mixtral on A100 80GB.
consequences: Compute budget reference for replication.

label: Experimental questions
verbatim: (i) Can Router KD consistently recover performance lost due to MoE compression? (ii) Does its effectiveness generalize across different compression paradigms? (iii) How does MoE architecture influence the benefits of router calibration?
paraphrase: Three experimental questions framing §5.
preconditions: Two backbones × three paradigms × two methods each.
consequences: Defines structure of results in §5.4–§5.5.

label: Baseline list
verbatim: For Expert Pruning baselines, we adopt REAP ... and CFES .... For Expert Editing, we utilize MoBE ... Additionally, we include TDMoE .... For Expert Merging, we select HC-SMoE ... [and] M-SMoE.
paraphrase: Baselines: Pruning={REAP, CFES}; Editing={MoBE, TD-MoE}; Merging={HC-SMoE, M-SMoE}.
consequences: Defines six target methods Router KD is applied on top of.

label: MoBE backprop caveat
verbatim: Although MoBE involves backpropagation and thus may not be strictly classified as retraining-free, we adopt it as a suitable baseline because it is significantly more computationally efficient than standard fine-tuning and does not require large-scale training datasets for compression.
paraphrase: MoBE uses backprop but is treated as retraining-free here for being lightweight and small-data.
preconditions: Compressed-stage backprop accepted as 'retraining-free' under this paper's definition.
consequences: Sets the bar for what counts as retraining-free in spec.

label: Benchmark suite
verbatim: we used BBH ... CoQA ... GSM8k, GSM8k Platinum, MATH, AIME 1983-2024 ..., and AIME 2025. ... MBPP and HumanEval-Instruct ... CoT reasoning capabilities on the BBH, GSM8k, and GSM8k Platinum datasets ... nine benchmark datasets ... to assess Multiple Choice Question Answering (MCQA) performance.
paraphrase: Benchmark suite: reasoning/QA (BBH fewshot/zeroshot, CoQA), math (GSM8k, GSM8k-Platinum, MATH, AIME 1983-2024, AIME 2025), code (MBPP, HumanEval-Instruct), CoT variants, and 9 MCQA datasets (ARC, HellaSwag, MedMCQA, MedQA, OpenBookQA, PIQA, WinoGrande, MMLU, etc.).
consequences: Comprehensive evaluation across QA/math/code/MCQA.

label: Eval stack & decoding
verbatim: All experiments were evaluated using the lm-evaluation-harness ... and vLLM .... Qwen3 was tested in an environment equipped with NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs, while Mixtral was tested with A100 80GB GPUs. The random seed was fixed at 42 for all experiments, and all benchmarks were measured using greedy decoding with a temperature of 0.
paraphrase: Eval: lm-eval-harness + vLLM; seed 42; greedy decoding (temp 0); A100-40GB for Qwen3, A100-80GB for Mixtral.
consequences: Reproducibility anchors for replication.

label: Expert retention rate
verbatim: A uniform expert retention rate of 62.5% was applied; in the context of pruning, this entails a reduction in experts from 128 to 80 for Qwen3 and from 8 to 5 for Mixtral.
paraphrase: Expert retention 62.5% uniformly: Qwen3 128→80, Mixtral 8→5.
consequences: Fixed compression ratio for cross-method comparison.

label: Router KD calibration data
verbatim: For all baselines, Router KD used the same C4 (Dodge et al., 2021) dataset, and the hyperparameters were set identically.
paraphrase: Router KD calibration set is C4 across all baselines, with shared hyperparameters.
consequences: Eliminates calibration-data confound across baselines.

label: Main result: Qwen3
verbatim: When applying Router KD to the Qwen3 backbone, we observed improvements across the majority of benchmarks. This trend was consistent across Expert Pruning, Editing, and Merging.
paraphrase: On Qwen3, Router KD improves majority of benchmarks across all three paradigms.
consequences: Validates Q1 (recovery) and Q2 (generality).

label: Coarse-grained limitation
verbatim: For Qwen3, Router KD improved benchmark scores across all six compression methodologies; conversely, the performance gains were relatively marginal for Mixtral.
paraphrase: Router KD strongly improves all six methods on Qwen3, but gains are marginal on Mixtral.
preconditions: Qwen3 (128 experts/layer fine-grained) vs Mixtral (8 experts/layer coarse-grained).
consequences: Architecture caveat: less benefit on coarse MoEs.

label: Catastrophic-collapse failure mode
verbatim: Router KD yields no performance improvements when the model suffers from catastrophic collapse—that is, when benchmark scores drop to near-zero immediately after compression. This suggests that in catastrophic collapse, the failure likely stems from damage beyond misrouting (e.g., degraded expert representations), which router-only calibration cannot recover.
paraphrase: If post-compression scores collapse to ~0, Router KD cannot recover; failure is in expert representations, not routing.
consequences: Spec must avoid using Router KD as a fallback for collapsed models.

label: Compression-improves-performance edge case
verbatim: upon investigating cases where Router KD led to performance regression, we found that these often coincided with instances where compression paradoxically improved model performance. ... Router KD becomes counterproductive because the Teacher (the original model) effectively underperforms compared to the Student (the compressed model).
paraphrase: When compression paradoxically improves a benchmark, Router KD regresses because the teacher is now worse than the student.
preconditions: Compression rarely improves a benchmark; teacher>student normally holds.
consequences: Possible regression cases; not common.

label: Routing-space size |Ω| = C(E,k)
verbatim: Under top-k routing with E experts, the number of distinct expert subsets is |Ω| = C(E,k). For Mixtral-style routing (E=8, k=2), |Ω| = C(8,2) = 28, whereas for fine-grained MoEs (e.g., E=128, k=8), |Ω| = C(128,8) ≈ 1.43 × 10^12.
paraphrase: Number of top-k subsets = C(E,k). Mixtral: 28; Qwen3: ~1.43e12.
consequences: Explains why fine-grained MoEs have far more routing flexibility for KD to exploit.

label: Eq. 4 (KL gradient w.r.t. student logit)
paraphrase: Per-expert gradient of KL on routing distribution is (1/τ)(g_S,e − g_T,e); separable across experts.
consequences: Signal vanishes once student matches dominant teacher experts.

label: Eq. 5 (Linear router weight gradient)
paraphrase: If router is linear (z_S = W_S x), gradient w.r.t. router weights is outer product of routing-error and input.
consequences: Router has only E adjustment coordinates; small E limits KD signal.

label: Coarse vs fine teacher routing entropy
verbatim: In coarse-grained MoEs, experts typically have broad coverage, and the teacher routing distribution is often highly concentrated (low entropy), which means H(g^{coarse}_T) ≪ H(g^{fine}_T). When g_T is already near-hard, (g_S − g_T) quickly becomes small once the student matches the dominant experts, which deprives the gradients of informative 'dark knowledge' regarding non-selected experts.
paraphrase: Coarse-grained MoE teachers have near-hard routing distributions, so KD gradient vanishes after dominant experts match — limited dark knowledge.
consequences: Theoretical reason Mixtral benefits less.

label: Fine-grained MoE dark-knowledge advantage
verbatim: in fine-grained MoEs with large E and E ≫ k, the routing space is vastly larger and the teacher targets are often less peaky, so KD provides richer 'dark knowledge' over many non-selected experts, acting as a navigational cue across a substantially more complex decision boundary.
paraphrase: Large E, E≫k → smoother teacher router → KD has more informative gradients over many non-selected experts.
consequences: Justifies stronger Router-KD recovery on fine-grained models.

label: §5.5 ratio robustness
verbatim: to verify that the observed gains are not limited to a particular compression setting (e.g., 62.5%), we applied a milder compression configuration to Qwen3, retaining 75% of the total parameters, and then evaluated the effect of Router KD. ... Router KD exhibited pronounced improvements across all benchmark categories and mitigated the performance drop from the original model compared to the non-KD baseline.
paraphrase: At 75% retention on Qwen3, Router KD still gives consistent recovery; results in Table 6.
consequences: Router KD is not tied to 62.5% setting.

label: Table 1 — Calibration dataset
verbatim: Calibration dataset = c4
paraphrase: Router KD always uses C4 as calibration corpus.
consequences: Single calibration source; no domain-specific mixture.

label: Table 1 — Epochs
verbatim: Epochs = 1
paraphrase: Router KD trains for exactly 1 epoch over the calibration data.
consequences: One pass; explains short wall-clock.

label: Table 1 — Batch size
verbatim: Batch size = 2
paraphrase: Per-step batch size is 2.
consequences: Spec's bs=8 corresponds to effective (post-accumulation) batch.

label: Table 1 — Gradient accumulation
verbatim: Gradient accumulation steps = 4
paraphrase: Gradient accumulation = 4 steps.
preconditions: Combined with bs=2 yields effective batch size 8.
consequences: Effective batch = 8 (matches spec §5.5 bs=8 statement).

label: Table 1 — Learning rate
verbatim: Learning rate = 5 × 10^{-5}
paraphrase: Constant LR of 5e-5 for Router KD across all experiments. (No schedule specified in Table 1.)
preconditions: Optimizer not explicitly named in main text; default AdamW assumed.
consequences: Spec must use 5e-5; LR schedule unspecified — paper appears to use constant LR with 1 epoch.

label: Table 1 — Max sequence length
verbatim: Max sequence length = 512
paraphrase: Calibration sequences are truncated/packed to length 512.
consequences: Token budget per step = 2 × 512 = 1024.

label: Table 1 — KD temperature
verbatim: KD temperature (T) = 1.0
paraphrase: KD temperature τ = 1.0 (effectively no softening).
preconditions: Eq. 2 with τ=1; Eq. 3 prefactor τ²=1.
consequences: Pure forward-KL on raw next-token distributions.

label: Table 1 — Max calibration samples
verbatim: Max calibration samples = 3000
paraphrase: Up to 3000 calibration samples used.
preconditions: Single epoch over up to 3000 sequences.
consequences: Total update steps ≈ 3000/(bs=2)/(gradaccum=4) ≈ 375 optimizer steps.

label: Router vs expert distinction
verbatim: We freeze θ_E and optimize only θ_R ... gradients are backpropagated and applied exclusively to the student router parameters θ_R, while all expert and backbone parameters remain frozen.
paraphrase: Distillation only updates router (gating) parameters θ_R; experts θ_E (incl. compressed/edited/merged experts) and backbone are frozen.
consequences: Distinguishes 'router KD' from full-model KD.

label: KL vs CE choice
verbatim: We then minimize the token-level KL divergence from teacher to student, masking padding tokens.
paraphrase: Loss is forward KL D_KL(p_T ‖ p_S), not cross-entropy on hard labels nor reverse KL.
consequences: Spec must use forward-KL on softened distributions; no CE term.

label: Table 2 — Qwen3 main results header
verbatim: Table 2. Performance comparison of compression methods with Router KD on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507. This table compares ... [REAP, MoBE, HC-SMoE × Router KD on/off]
paraphrase: Table 2 reports Qwen3 results for REAP, MoBE, HC-SMoE with and without Router KD across the full benchmark suite.
consequences: Primary numerical evidence cited by paper.

label: Table 3 — Qwen3 alternative baselines
verbatim: Table 3. Performance comparison of alternative compression baselines with Router KD on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507. We evaluate additional baselines for each category: CFES (Pruning), TD-MoE (Editing), and M-SMoE (Merging).
paraphrase: Table 3: CFES, TD-MoE, M-SMoE on Qwen3 ± Router KD; consistent with Table 2 trends.
consequences: Generality across multiple methods per paradigm.

label: Table 4 — Mixtral main results
verbatim: Table 4. Performance comparison of compression methods with Router KD on Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1.
paraphrase: Table 4: REAP, MoBE, HC-SMoE on Mixtral ± Router KD; gains marginal vs Qwen3.
consequences: Quantifies coarse-grained limitation.

label: Table 5 — Mixtral alternative baselines
verbatim: Table 5. Performance comparison of alternative compression baselines with Router KD on Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct-v0.1. Evaluation of CFES, TD-MoE, and M-SMoE on the Mixtral backbone.
paraphrase: Table 5: CFES, TD-MoE, M-SMoE on Mixtral ± Router KD.
consequences: Confirms coarse-grained ceiling holds across method families.

label: Table 6 — 75% retention robustness
verbatim: Table 6. Robustness analysis of Router KD under milder compression (75% Parameter Retention) on Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507.
paraphrase: Table 6 shows Router KD still helps at 75% retention on Qwen3.
consequences: Recovery is not specific to 62.5%.

label: CFES baseline batch size
verbatim: CFES (Expert Pruning). ... batch size=32 ... max length=4096 ... prune method=c2f
paraphrase: CFES pruning baseline uses batch size 32, max length 4096, coarse-to-fine prune method, segment-mean features, l2 metric. Same on Qwen3 and Mixtral.
consequences: Replication recipe for the CFES baseline.

label: MoBE training hyperparams
verbatim: MoBE ... --num epochs 10000  --batch size 32  --num batches 4  --learning rate 0.07  --activation "silu"  --truncation 768 [Qwen3] / 1672 [Mixtral]; Num B=8 (Qwen3) / Num B=2 (Mixtral)
paraphrase: MoBE factorization uses 10000 epochs, batch 32, 4 num-batches, lr 0.07, SiLU; truncation 768/1672; basis count 8/2 for Qwen3/Mixtral.
consequences: These are MoBE-internal training hyperparams (independent of Router KD).

label: HC-SMoE merging settings
verbatim: HC-SMoE ... DOMINANT="no", SIM BASE="expert-output", MERGE METHOD="zipit", MODE="normal", Number SENTENCES=4 [Qwen3] / 32 [Mixtral], max block size=2048, TRAIN Batch Size=2, START LAYER=0, GROUP LIMIT=4, CLUSTER METHOD="hierarchical", LINKAGE METHOD="average", STOP METRIC="silhouette", INGREDIENT="act"
paraphrase: HC-SMoE: no dominant expert, expert-output similarity, zipit merge, hierarchical/average linkage, silhouette stop, INGREDIENT=act, 4 (Qwen3) or 32 (Mixtral) calibration sentences.
consequences: Merging baseline recipe.

label: TD-MoE settings
verbatim: TD-MoE ... --whitening nsamples 256  --cluster type "global"  --model seq len 2048  --whiten type "output"  --ratio 0.4  --decomposition method "svd"; LAYERS TO COMPRESS = {4,5,7,8,9,10,11,13,14,15,18,33,34,35,36,37,38,39} [Qwen3] / (3,5,6,7,9,10,12,22,23,24,25,26) [Mixtral]
paraphrase: TD-MoE Tucker/SVD whitened decomposition, ratio 0.4, global cluster, 256 whitening samples, seq 2048, output whitening, SVD decomposition; specific MoE-layer index lists per backbone.
consequences: Editing baseline recipe.

label: M-SMoE merging settings
verbatim: M-SMoE ... --similarity base="router-logits"  --subset ratio=0.01  block size = 512 [Qwen3] / 512 [Mixtral]  batch size = 1  num fewshot = 5
paraphrase: M-SMoE merges with router-logit similarity, 1% subset, block size 512, batch 1, 5 fewshot.
consequences: Second merging baseline recipe.

label: REAP calibration recipe
verbatim: REAP ... we adopted the same calibration data recipe used in the REAP paper: a 50/50 mixture of allenai/c4 and theblackcat102/evol-codealpaca-v1.
paraphrase: REAP pruning baseline uses 50/50 C4 + evol-codealpaca-v1 calibration mix (per REAP defaults).
consequences: Note: REAP's compression-stage calibration ≠ Router KD calibration (which is C4-only).

label: Contributions
verbatim: We propose a taxonomy of retraining-free MoE compression—Expert Pruning, Editing, and Merging—and identify router miscalibration as a primary source of post-compression performance degradation. ... We introduce Router Knowledge Distillation as a lightweight and general recovery strategy that updates only router parameters.
paraphrase: Two contributions: (1) three-paradigm taxonomy + identification of router miscalibration as primary degradation cause; (2) Router KD recovery method.
preconditions: Theoretical + empirical analysis of routing discrepancy.
consequences: Frames the spec's interpretation of 'retraining-free'.

label: Redefinition of retraining-free
verbatim: fully retraining-free compression—defined as leaving both experts and router untouched—is often impractical for achieving strong performance. Instead, we advocate a more precise interpretation: avoiding updates to expert parameters, while allowing lightweight router recalibration.
paraphrase: Paper redefines 'retraining-free' to mean 'expert-frozen, router-recalibratable' rather than 'no updates at all'.
preconditions: Original strict definition leaves both experts and router untouched.
consequences: Spec inherits this looser definition; Router KD-style updates allowed.

label: Routers differ even when expert sets match (pruning)
verbatim: even if the selected experts match exactly pre- and post-pruning, the router's output values are unlikely to be identical. Because MoE LLMs are multi-layered, for the router outputs to be perfectly identical, this best-case scenario must be satisfied in every layer, which is statistically improbable. Therefore, it can be trivially stated that g̃^{orig,(A)}_i(x) ≠ g̃^{pruned,(A)}_i(x).
paraphrase: Multi-layer MoEs almost surely violate per-layer best-case assumption, so renormalized router weights differ even with identical S sets.
preconditions: Multi-layer MoE; statistical independence across layers.
consequences: Router calibration always needed.

label: Editing best-case bound
verbatim: |y_orig(x) − y^{best}_edit(x)| ≤ Σ_{i∈S} |g̃^{orig,(S)}_i(x) E_i(x) − g̃^{edit,(S)}_i(x) X_i(x)|
paraphrase: Triangle-inequality bound on best-case editing output difference summed over original expert set.
consequences: Bounds editing-induced output drift.

label: Pruning > editing/merging in router drift
verbatim: Pruning models exhibit a relatively larger divergence in assigned probabilities compared to Editing and Merging. This is attributed to the fact that Pruning drops experts and masks the corresponding indices, whereas Editing and Merging retain the total number of experts, thereby preserving the dimensionality of the router.
paraphrase: Pruning has biggest router-prob divergence because dropping experts changes router-output dimensionality; editing/merging keep dim intact.
consequences: Prioritizes router calibration for pruning paradigm.

label: Top-8 overlap depth degradation
verbatim: as the layers deepen, the router increasingly selects experts different from those intended by the original model.
paraphrase: Top-8 expert-selection overlap with the original drops monotonically with layer depth.
consequences: Router drift accumulates layerwise; deep layers most affected.

label: Post-merge router calibration discussion
verbatim: we study parameter-level MoE compression (e.g., expert pruning, merging, and editing), where experts are structurally modified or removed. In this regime, the pre-trained router is often no longer well-matched to the post-compression expert set, making router–expert mismatch a key driver of performance degradation. Our findings therefore support a broader conclusion: effective MoE compression requires not only modifying experts, but also calibrating the router to remain consistent with the altered expert landscape.
paraphrase: Explicit post-compression (incl. post-merge) router-calibration mandate: changed expert landscape requires aligning router; Router KD is the proposed mechanism.
consequences: Spec §5.5: post-merge router calibration step required.

label: Related: EAC-MoE TopK-MSE router cal
verbatim: EAC-MoE attributes a major failure mode of quantized MoEs to expert-shift—a distortion of expert selection after quantization—and proposes router calibration to mitigate the cumulative accumulation of expert selection shift across layers, using a TopK-MSE objective that focuses alignment on the experts most likely to be selected.
paraphrase: EAC-MoE (cited): router calibration via TopK-MSE on expert selection alleviates quantization-induced expert shift.
consequences: Alternative router-calibration objective beyond Router KD.

label: Related: GEMQ global router fine-tune
verbatim: GEMQ observes that quantization substantially distorts router behavior and shows that merely reusing full-precision router signals is insufficient; instead, a lightweight global router fine-tuning step (updating only router parameters) provides substantial gains in perplexity and downstream accuracy under low-bit regimes.
paraphrase: GEMQ (cited): post-quantization global router fine-tune (router-only) is necessary; reusing FP router fails.
consequences: Independent confirmation that router-only updates are high-leverage.

label: Pruning amplifies in common/worst cases
verbatim: this difference will inevitably amplify in the Most Common and Worst scenarios, where the selected experts differ from those in the original model. This suggests that the divergence between the pruned and original models is, in part, attributable to the router.
paraphrase: When the pruned router selects different experts than the original, the divergence between models is even larger; partially attributable to router.
consequences: Router calibration becomes more impactful at higher compression ratios.

label: Editing diverges with imperfection
verbatim: the greater the imperfection in the Expert Editing method, the more the router's output diverges from the original, inevitably altering the set of selected experts.
paraphrase: Editing fidelity X_i ↔ E_i directly controls how much the router selects different experts.
consequences: Imperfect editing → router drift → router calibration needed.

label: Q3 architectural answer
verbatim: the beneficial impact of Router KD is diminished in coarse-grained MoE models like Mixtral.
paraphrase: Answer to Q3: architecture matters; coarse-grained MoEs (small E) benefit less.
consequences: Spec applicability skewed toward fine-grained MoEs.

label: Frozen-experts protocol
verbatim: we propose Router Knowledge Distillation (Router KD), a lightweight distillation procedure that updates only the student router parameters while keeping all other student parameters frozen.
paraphrase: Router KD freezes all non-router student parameters.
consequences: Spec must implement freezing of θ_E during KD.

label: Loss-mask normalizer N_x
verbatim: Let m_{t+1} ∈ {0,1} be the loss mask for the target position (t+1), and define the normalizer N_x = Σ_{t=1}^{L−1} m_{t+1} + ε, where ε is a small constant.
paraphrase: Per-sequence normalizer counts non-padding next-token positions plus ε for stability.
consequences: Each sequence's KL is averaged over its valid next-token positions.

label: Soft-gating gradient pathway
verbatim: we follow standard MoE routing implementations in which gradients flow through the (soft) gating weights of the selected experts during the forward pass, but parameter updates are restricted to the router.
paraphrase: Gradients flow through soft top-k gate weights; only router weights receive updates.
preconditions: Standard differentiable MoE routing (e.g., HF transformers' MoE layer).
consequences: Top-k indices not differentiable; soft weights of chosen experts carry the signal.

label: Hybrid methods excluded
verbatim: Hybrid compression methods are intentionally excluded to isolate the independent effect of router calibration.
paraphrase: No hybrid (e.g., prune+edit) baselines; pure single-paradigm methods only.
consequences: Cleaner attribution of Router KD effect.

label: Effective batch size = 8 (matches spec bs=8)
verbatim: Batch size 2 ... Gradient accumulation steps 4
paraphrase: Effective batch size for Router KD = batch_size × grad_accum = 2 × 4 = 8. (This matches the spec's stated bs=8 recommendation.)
consequences: Spec's bs=8 is consistent with paper's effective batch.

label: No LR schedule specified
verbatim: Parameter Value | Calibration dataset c4 | Epochs 1 | Batch size 2 | Gradient accumulation steps 4 | Learning rate 5 × 10−5 | Max sequence length 512 | KD temperature (T) 1.0 | Max calibration samples 3000
paraphrase: Table 1 lists all Router KD hyperparams; no LR scheduler, warmup, weight decay, or optimizer is mentioned — implying defaults (constant LR, AdamW, no warmup) over 1 epoch.
consequences: Spec must replicate without warmup/cosine schedule, or document divergence.

label: Inference decoding
verbatim: all benchmarks were measured using greedy decoding with a temperature of 0.
paraphrase: Eval-time decoding is greedy (T=0); seed=42.
consequences: Deterministic eval; no sampling-based variance.

label: Shared Router-KD hyperparams across cases
verbatim: We used exactly the same Router KD hyperparameters for every experiment case and model variant, and consistently used c4 as the calibration dataset; only the teacher–student pair (i.e., the backbone and the compressed baseline) was changed.
paraphrase: Single Router-KD hyperparam configuration for every (backbone, baseline) pair.
consequences: Strong generality claim; no per-method tuning.

label: Qwen3 hardware
verbatim: Qwen3 was tested in an environment equipped with NVIDIA A100 40GB GPUs
paraphrase: Qwen3 experiments use A100 40GB GPUs.
consequences: Hardware footprint reference.

label: Mixtral hardware
verbatim: Mixtral was tested with A100 80GB GPUs.
paraphrase: Mixtral experiments use A100 80GB GPUs.
consequences: Hardware footprint reference.

label: Figure 3 summary
verbatim: Performance Impact of Router KD on Qwen3 vs. Mixtral. The chart compares performance recovery across Pruning, Editing, and Merging, where green bars indicate improved benchmarks. Router KD proves significantly more effective for the fine-grained Qwen3 (Left) compared to the coarse-grained Mixtral (Right).
paraphrase: Fig 3 visualizes per-benchmark wins/losses; Qwen3 shows mostly green (improvements), Mixtral mostly mixed.
consequences: Visual summary of main result.

label: Perfect-routing improbability
verbatim: for the router outputs to be perfectly identical, this best-case scenario must be satisfied in every layer, which is statistically improbable.
paraphrase: Probability that every layer is in best-case scenario simultaneously is vanishing in deep MoE.
consequences: Theoretical justification that real models are always in 'common' or 'worst' case.

label: No expert-set / gate-dim sharing required
verbatim: Router KD avoids requiring the teacher and student to share identical expert sets or gate dimensionalities, which may not hold after pruning, editing, or merging.
paraphrase: KD on next-token logits is invariant to mismatched expert counts and router dimensions, unlike router-output matching.
preconditions: Teacher and student share vocabulary V.
consequences: Pruning (E_T ≠ E_S) and merging (N→M) are supported uniformly.
